elements.sent-2411-001: Tensors are ubiquitous in statistics and data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-002: The central object that links data science to tensor theory and algebra is that of a model with latent variables.
elements.sent-2411-003: We provide an overview of tensor theory, with a particular emphasis on its applications in algebraic statistics.
elements.sent-2411-004: This high-level treatment is supported by numerous examples to illustrate key concepts.
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, an extensive literature review is included to guide readers toward more detailed studies on the subject.
elements.sent-2411-006: Tensors, which extend matrices to higher dimensions, were introduced in the late 19th century by authors such as William Rowan Hamilton, Woldemar Voigt and Gregorio Ricci-Curbastro, being made widely accessible by the work \cite{Riccilevicivita .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Although tensors were developed in differential calculus and geometry, nowadays tensors play a crucial role in statistics and data science as they offer a powerful mathematical framework for understanding complex phenomena, capturing intricate relationships, and extracting valuable insights from high-dimensional datasets (see the survey \cite{jietal19 for instance).}
elements.sent-2411-008: In this overview paper, we explore the significance of tensors in statistics and data science, focusing on their mathematical foundations and connections to algebraic statistics.. Basic applications.
elements.sent-2411-009: Tensors find extensive usage across various domains, including machine learning, neuroscience, and genomics.
elements.sent-2411-010: In machine learning, tensors facilitate the analysis of multidimensional data, enabling tasks such as image recognition, video summarization, and natural language processing.
elements.sent-2411-011: For example, in image recognition, tensors are used to represent high-resolution images, capturing spatial relationships and enabling the development of sophisticated deep learning models (see, e.g., \cite{Hartley2004,Bertolini24 ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: In neuroscience, tensors play a crucial role in unraveling brain connectivity patterns and understanding the functional organization of the brain (see for instance \cite{Cong15 ).}
elements.sent-2411-013: Brain networks, represented as higher-order tensors, capture the interactions among multiple brain regions, providing insights into cognitive processes, neurological disorders, and brain plasticity.
elements.sent-2411-014: Tensors enable the extraction of meaningful features from functional magnetic resonance imaging data, helping researchers identify brain regions associated with specific tasks or conditions, \cite{calamante2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Genomics is another domain where tensors offer a powerful framework for analyzing high-dimensional biological data.
elements.sent-2411-016: In multiomics studies, tensors are employed to integrate diverse molecular characteristics, such as gene expression, DNA methylation, and histone modifications (see for example \cite{Yang2018,Nafees20,Schreiber20 ) .}
elements.sent-2411-017: By modeling these complex relationships using tensors, researchers can identify molecular signatures associated with diseases, predict patient outcomes, and guide personalized treatment strategies.. Tensor modelling.
elements.sent-2411-018: The geometric complexity of tensors poses challenges in analysis and interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-019: To overcome this, researchers have developed methods that exploit simpler tensor structures.
elements.sent-2411-020: Many models considered rely on more basic building blocks: diagonal tensors and rank-one tensors or low rank tensors.
elements.sent-2411-021: This simplification enhances interpretability and facilitates efficient computation and inference in high-dimensional settings.
elements.sent-2411-022: As we will see, both the diagonal and the rank-one tensors arise naturally in the context of statistical independence.
elements.sent-2411-023: More generally, many of the probabilistic models that build on independence or conditional independence (e.g. Independence Component Analysis, Probabilistic Principal Component Analysis, Naive Bayes models) are based on finding a tensor decomposition into lower rank terms.
elements.sent-2411-024: The field of algebraic statistics has emerged as a valuable tool for studying tensors within statistical models (bibliography on algebraic statistics includes the books \cite{Pistone2000, ASCB2005, drton2008lectures, sullivantbook, zwiernik2015semialgebraic ).}
elements.sent-2411-025: By leveraging algebraic concepts, it provides insights into the properties and behavior of tensors in various statistical contexts.
elements.sent-2411-026: Algebraic tools have been successfully applied to address challenges related to the identifiability of latent variable models, parameter estimation, model fitting, design of experiments, and other fundamental statistical problems.
elements.sent-2411-028: In data science, including latent variables (or hidden, unobserved) in the analysis is a popular way of improving expressibility of a model without dramatically increasing its computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-029: Despite their immense potential, the presence of latent variables introduces challenges in model estimation and interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-030: Addressing these challenges often requires sophisticated algorithms and statistical techniques.
elements.sent-2411-031: This formed an important motivation behind the development of the singular learning theory \citep{watanabe2009algebraic and large part of recent development in algebraic statistics is centered around related problems.}
elements.sent-2411-032: By harnessing the algebraic structure of tensors, researchers have gained a deeper understanding of statistical models with latent variables and developed efficient estimation algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-033: Modelling dependence between random variables (both observed and unobserved) is usually done via a graphical model.
elements.sent-2411-034: On a graphical model, the vertices of a graph represent random variables and its edges encode the conditional dependence relations.
elements.sent-2411-035: When this representation uses directed edges on an acyclic graph, the model is also known as a Bayesian network or directed acyclic graph model.
elements.sent-2411-036: The models for undirected graphs are sometimes called Markov random fields.
elements.sent-2411-037: Latent trees form the most tractable family of Bayesian networks with unobserved variables, which can be used to model dependence structures when unobserved confounders are expected ~\cite[see for example][Section 2]{pearl2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: However, there are several other reasons why latent tree models have become popular across sciences.
elements.sent-2411-039: First, latent tree models encompass a broader range of probability distributions compared to fully-observed tree models, while still leveraging their computational benefits, especially in high-dimensional scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-040: Additionally, the max-product algorithm enables efficient inference of unobserved states.
elements.sent-2411-041: Second, trees can represent evolutionary processes and, in this context they are used in phylogenetic analysis (see ~\cite{SteelPhylogeny and Section \ref{sec:phylo , in linguistics~\citep{ringe2002indo,shiers2014gaussian , modeling language evolution, and in network tomography~\citep{castro2004network,eriksson2010toward for inferring internet structures.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Gaussian latent tree models (e.g., Brownian motion tree models) naturally capture correlations for network tomography due to correlations diminishing with tree distance; refer to Section~\ref{sec:gaussian .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Third, latent tree models capture hierarchical structure in complex datasets, and they are closely related to various hierarchical clustering methods \citep{bishop1998hierarchical,lawrence2004gaussian,zhangCluster,mourad2013survey .}
elements.sent-2411-044: This framework finds also application in computer vision as in~\cite{willsky2002multiresolution and \cite{choi2010exploiting .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Latent tree models generalize hidden Markov models (HMMs), which are defined on caterpillar trees~\cite{rabiner1989tutorial .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Hidden Markov Tree models (HMTMs) \cite{crouse1998wavelet relax HMM's restrictions, admitting any unobserved tree structure.}
elements.sent-2411-048: This inclusiveness extends to phylogenetic models, Naive Bayes models, Brownian motion tree models, and one factor analysis model, leading to a unified framework for diverse model classes~\cite{wainwright2008graphical .}
elements.sent-2411-049: An important example of latent variable model is Factor Analysis, which is commonly employed in the study of multivariate data to identify underlying latent factors that influence the observed variables.
elements.sent-2411-050: It finds applications in finance, psychology, and social sciences among others.
elements.sent-2411-051: A related model class is given by Principal Component Analysis (PCA) models, a widely used technique for dimensionality reduction and data compression.
elements.sent-2411-052: The probabilistic version, known as probabilistic PCA, introduces latent variables to account for the noise in the data, making it more flexible in real-world applications, see Section \ref{sec:ic .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Another important class of graphical models with latent variables is the Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs, see Section \ref{sec:RBM ).}
elements.sent-2411-054: They serve as crucial components in deep learning, particularly in training deep belief networks and generative models.
elements.sent-2411-055: RBMs involve latent variables that capture hidden patterns in the data and can be extended to deep probabilistic graphical models with multiple layers of latent variables.
elements.sent-2411-056: They have had a transformative impact on fields like computer vision and speech recognition.
elements.sent-2411-057: From an algebraic standpoint, this model class has been thoroughly examined by Mont\'{u far and collaborators \cite{montufar2015discrete,montufar2018restricted .. Summary.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: In this expository paper we explain the role of tensors in algebraic statistics by showcasing several examples.
elements.sent-2411-059: In Section~\ref{sec:defs we present tensors that arise naturally in statistics and present the first results that give answers to statistical questions in terms of tensor properties.}
elements.sent-2411-060: In Section~\ref{sec:GMs we give a brief overview of graphical models and depict examples of both directed and undirected graphical models with either discrete or continuous variables by putting special emphasis in latent tree models.}
elements.sent-2411-061: We devote Section \ref{sec:gaussian to gaussian variables, which needs a special treatment.}
elements.sent-2411-062: By building upon the previous examples, in Section~\ref{sec:tensorsAS we illustrate the concepts of tensor rank and tensor decomposition and explore a method of moments.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Finally, Section~\ref{sec:apps is devoted to further applications of tensors in algebraic statistics, where we focus on phylogenetic analysis, restricted Boltzmann machines, and structural equation models.}

elements.sent-2411-383: In Independent Component Analysis, it is postulated that the observed random vector $X=(X_1,\ldots,X_m)$ can be written as a linear transformation $A\varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon=(\varepsilon_1,\ldots,\varepsilon_m)$ has independent components and $A\in \R^{m\times m}$ is invertible.
